title: podman run takes 20 seconds to exit after container exits
body:
### Issue Description

When I run any podman command like: `podman run --rm --network=none -it alpine echo hello` it takes 20 seconds to return a prompt.  The container itself executes and exits immediately as expected, but podman does not return a prompt.  If I press 'Ctrl-C' while waiting, I get a prompt, but while the container is not running, it also hasn't been deleted.  
If I execute a 'podman stop -t -0 ...' on the container, it returns immediately

It doesn't matter if I use the `--rm` or not, the behavior is the same.  Same with the use of `--network=xxx`. Using `podman exec ...` on a running container exits immediately after execution as it should.  `podman stop` and `podman rm` both exit immediately.

I have tried using strace, but strace on the podman process shows nothing interesting, and the conmon process itself exits immediately as expected.

running  `strace -tt podman run --rm alpine echo 1`:
```
13:59:32.571596 rt_sigprocmask(SIG_SETMASK, [KILL STOP], NULL, 8) = 0
13:59:32.571694 futex(0xc00006c948, FUTEX_WAKE_PRIVATE, 1) = 1
13:59:32.571787 futex(0xc00006dd48, FUTEX_WAKE_PRIVATE, 1) = 1
13:59:32.571921 futex(0x5562c722a348, FUTEX_WAIT_PRIVATE, 0, NULL) = ?
13:59:53.008599 +++ exited with 0 +++
```

The 20 seconds is very consistent and seems to be some sort of timeout.

This only happens with one user on the system,  and does not seem to happen when that user runs podman through systemd user-mode.  I am pretty sure that if I reboot, the issue will go away, but I would like to root-cause if possible, as this is a production server, and I would like to not have a re-occurrence.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
```podman run --network=none alpine echo 1```


### Describe the results you received

Podman run hangs for 20 seconds

### Describe the results you expected

`podman run` should return a prompt immediately after container completes

### podman info output

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body: does the journal show any hints? Maybe looking at the events being logged and timing you might be able to determine where the time being spent is.

you should run with `strace -f` if you want to see anything useful.

The 20 second timeout is used by podman to wait for the exit code to be stored in the db. This does not seem to happen for some reason. Also adding `--log-level debug` to the podman command should show more info.

I thought that I had already tried `strace -f` but I was wrong.  However, it didn't provide any additional info besides continually waiting for the FUTEX.

I ran`podman --log-level debug run --rm --network=none alpine echo 1` and see:
```
INFO[0000] Failed to add conmon to cgroupfs sandbox cgroup: creating cgroup path conmon: open /sys/fs/cgroup/cgroup.subtree_control: permission denied
[conmon:d]: failed to write to /proc/self/oom_score_adj: Permission denied

DEBU[0000] Received: 4099378
INFO[0000] Got Conmon PID as 4099367
DEBU[0000] Created container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3 in OCI runtime
DEBU[0000] Attaching to container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3
DEBU[0000] Starting container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3 with command [echo 1]
DEBU[0000] Started container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3
DEBU[0000] Notify sent successfully
1
<...20 second wait here...>
DEBU[0021] Exceeded conmon timeout waiting for container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3 to exit
DEBU[0021] Checking if container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3 should restart
DEBU[0021] Removing container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3
DEBU[0021] Cleaning up container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3
DEBU[0021] Successfully cleaned up container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3
DEBU[0021] Unmounted container "e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3"
DEBU[0021] Removing all exec sessions for container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3
DEBU[0021] Container e3ba44328ba60d5741549dc0daa87818bf30369bff61e48990ab1a3867d9a4f3 storage is already unmounted, skipping...
DEBU[0021] Called run.PersistentPostRunE(podman --log-level debug run --rm --network=none alpine echo 1)
DEBU[0021] Shutting down engines
```
I validated that I get these same 2 failures at the top on a user that does not exhibit this issue.

when comparing `podman info` between a working and non-working user, I discovered that graphRoot was different.  I had moved the rootless containers to a dedicated volume, and set `rootless_storage_path="/mnt/containers/podman/users/$USER"` however this was not taking place for the 'broken' user which was still using storage from ~/.local/containers/storage

Thinking this may be related, I tried a lot of things to convince podman to use the `rootless_storage_path` to no avail.  I ended up rebooting to see if podman would use the proper container path (the answer is still no) but as expected, the reboot did indeed fix the hang condition so I can no longer debug this issue.

I will continue to try to determine why podman ignores the 'rootless_storage_path' for this specific issue (I do not have any overrides in ~/.config/containers) and file a new ticket on that issue if warranted.

I found that `podman --log-level debug info` showed my graphroot was being overridden, and I found https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17730 which shows that the bolt_state.db was he culprit.  If I remove the bolt_state.db file, the graphRoot is fixed, but all of my containers and volumes disappear.  It seems this issue is unrelated to the hang, so I'm closing this issue as it is now unreproducible.
